sections.title: D Pseudo Algorithms
sections.content: Concept Extraction The following pseudo-code outlines the Differentiative Concept Extraction phase of our framework. Because LLMs have limited context windows and output length constraints, we process data in batches and limit the active concept pool using threshold (set to 4 in our experiments). This limit is crucial, as too many concepts exponentially expand the text length in later steps. For each batch, the LLM first checks if the current concept set is sufficient to differentiate between the predicted classes. If not, it extracts new concepts to bridge the gap. To remove non-differentiative concepts, we prune the pool every 10 iterations. For task with classes, the LLM assigns each concept one of 2Y possible combinations. Two of these labels denoting either that concept never appears in any class, or that it appears equally across all classes are marked as non-differentiative. The remaining 2Y 2 labels represent valid discriminative combinations. concept is kept in the pool only if its frequency of receiving differentiative label exceeds predefined threshold, τ . Once this extraction and filtering process concludes on the training set, the final concept set is fixed and applied to the test set. Algorithm 1: Iterative Differentiative Concept Extraction Input: Training set Dtrain, Test set Dtest, threshold τ , max bound Output: Concept set Initialize concept set foreach iteration = 1, 2, . . . and batch Dtrain do if insufficiently describes then LLM_Propose(b) (ensuring B) end if mod 10 == 0 then {c DiscriminativePower(c, b) > τ } end end {c DiscriminativePower(c, Dtest) > τ } return Select_Top_Concepts(C, 5) MCMC Inspired Data Expansion The execution flow of the data expansion procedure is detailed in Algorithm 2. For each seed instance, the algorithm generates textual counterfactuals. At each step, it iterates over every concept C, uniformly samples target class Y, and chooses direction dx {MORE, LESS} according to whether is currently included in the subset mapped from the scalar concept value ϕ(x)[c]. The target LLM proposes counterfactual text, re-annotates it with concept vector, and provides its annotation rationale. proposal is accepted only if the target concept shifts in the requested direction and the number of non-target concepts that drift is at most ϵ. If proposal is rejected, recursive refinement re-prompts the LLM with the failure feedback for up to retries. Algorithm 2: LLM-Guided MCMC Data Expansion with Recursive Refinement Input :Seed dataset D, concept set C, class set Y, target LLM , annotation function ϕ, MCMC steps K, max retries R, drift threshold ϵ Output :Expanded counterfactual dataset Dmcmc Dmcmc foreach instance x(0) do x(0) for 1 to do foreach concept do Uniform(Y) ψ ϕ(x) subset mapped from ψ[c] if then else dx LESS dx MORE end f.GenerateCounterfactual(x, c, y, dx) ψ, reason f.Annotate(x) subset mapped from ψ[c] nerr (cid:80) aligned ((dx = MORE S) (dx = LESS / S)) if aligned and nerr ϵ then I[ψ[cj] = ψ[cj]] cj C{c} Dmcmc Dmcmc {x} else xrec RecursiveRefinement(x, ψ, x, c, y, dx, reason, 1) if xrec = Null then xrec Dmcmc Dmcmc {xrec} end end end end end Function RecursiveRefinement(xbase, ψbase, xcurr, c, y, dx, reason, r): if > then return Null end feedback ConstructFeedback(c, y, dx, reason) xnew f.RegenerateCounterfactual(xcurr, feedback) ψnew, new_reason f.Annotate(xnew) Snew subset mapped from ψnew[c] nerr (cid:80) aligned ((dx = MORE Snew) (dx = LESS / Snew)) if aligned and nerr ϵ then I[ψnew[cj] = ψbase[cj]] cj C{c} return xnew return RecursiveRefinement(xbase, ψbase, xnew, c, y, dx, new_reason, + 1) else end

sections.title: E Prompts
sections.content: This section details the prompt templates utilized across the various stages of our methodology. For brevity, we present the base templates tailored for the LIBERTY dataset as representative examples, with the exception of the Preliminary Expansion stage, which was applied exclusively to the LAJ dataset. The fundamental structure of these prompts remains highly consistent across all other datasets, with only minor adaptations introduced to accommodate dataset-specific input formats. Note that these templates are static; during execution, data instances are dynamically injected in batches via an automated Python pipeline. Within these templates, placeholders such as "direction" or "DX" are dynamically populated with either "more" or "less," depending on the experimental scenario. Asterisk-bound phrases denote dynamic placeholders that are programmatically replaced by the Python pipeline during execution. Prompt E.1: Prompt for Inference and Label Alignment stage data set : LIBERTY Role: You are medical symptom classification model. Input: You will be given dataset containing multiple items. Each item includes: patient description: free-text description of patients symptoms. item id: An integer representing the items original index in larger dataset. Task: For each item, classify the condition described in the patient description into exactly one of the following labels: MIGRAINE SINUSITIS INFLUENZA If the description is mixed or ambiguous, choose the most likely dominant condition. If no dominant condition can be identified, assign INFLUENZA by default. Output requirements (strict): Your output must contain two sections, in this exact order. Section 1 Reasoning: Provide brief explanation (12 sentences) for each item explaining why the chosen label was selected. Do not include JSON in this section. Section 2 Labeled Data (JSON): Output single valid JSON object suitable for saving as .json file. Rules for the JSON: Each key must be the item id converted to string. Each value must be an object with exactly two fields: "patient description": the original patient description, unchanged. "diagnosis": either "MIGRAINE", "SINUSITIS", or "INFLUENZA". Do not add comments. Do not add extra fields. Do not change the patient description text. Do not output multiple JSON objects. After the JSON object is closed, print exactly: END JSON Example output: { "101": { "patient description": "Severe throbbing headache on the right side with nausea and sensitivity to light.", "diagnosis": "MIGRAINE" }, "102": { "patient description": "Facial pressure, congestion, and thick nasal discharge for several days.", "diagnosis": "SINUSITIS" }, "103": { "patient description": "Sudden fever, muscle aches, cough, and extreme fatigue.", "diagnosis": "INFLUENZA" } } END JSON Dataset: Prompt E.2: Prompt for first iteration of concept Differentiative Concept Extraction stage dataset : LIBERTY Role: You are an analyst extracting dataset-level features for medical condition differentiation. Input: You will receive dataset of patient cases. Each item contains: patient_description: free-text description of patients symptoms disease: one of the labels "MIGRAINE", "SINUSITIS", or "INFLUENZA" (these labels were assigned previously) Task: Symptom Extraction Across the entire dataset, extract exactly "B#" DISTINCT SYMPTOMS that help distinguish between the different diseases. Definition of Symptom: Symptom is concrete, clinically meaningful manifestation explicitly described in patient text (e.g., headache, fever, nausea, nasal congestion). Symptoms should be observable or reportable experiences, not abstract properties or disease names. Requirements: Differentiation: Each symptom must clearly distinguish between at least two of the diseases. For every symptom, it must be true that the symptom is typically more frequent, more prominent, or more characteristic in one disease than in at least one of the other diseases. Classification usefulness: The symptoms must be phrased so they can be identified in new patient descriptions whose disease label is unknown. Count: Extract exactly "B#" symptoms (no more, no less). Do NOT use the number of symptoms from any toy/exampleuse only "B#". Naming: Give each symptom short, precise name, preferably single word or short clinical phrase (e.g., Headache, Fever, Nausea, Congestion). Avoid trivial or circular symptoms: Do not list disease names or symptoms that simply restate the diagnosis (e.g., "Migraine pain"). Focus on concrete symptoms that generalize across patients. Output format (strict): Your output must contain two sections, in this exact order. Section 1 Reasoning: Briefly explain (13 sentences per symptom) why each symptom helps distinguish between the diseases based on patterns observed across the dataset. Section 2 Symptoms JSON: Output single valid JSON object suitable for saving as .json file. Each key is symptom name. Each value is concise description explaining how the symptom differs across MIGRAINE, SINUSITIS, and/or INFLUENZA. After the JSON object is closed, print exactly: END JSON Toy Example (for illustration only): Toy Input Dataset (number of symptoms to extract = 2): Item 1: patient_description: "Severe headache with nausea and sensitivity to light." disease: MIGRAINE Item 2: patient_description: "Facial pressure and nasal congestion lasting several days." disease: SINUSITIS Item 3: patient_description: "High fever, muscle aches, and extreme fatigue." disease: INFLUENZA Toy Output (number of symptoms to extract = 2): Reasoning: Headache: Head pain is described across diseases but differs in accompanying features and prominence, making it useful for differentiation. Fever: Fever appears primarily in systemic illness descriptions and is absent or rare in others, providing strong distinguishing signal. { "Headache": "Head pain is described across diseases but varies in context and co-occurring symptoms.", "Fever": "Fever appears mainly in systemic illness descriptions and is uncommon in others." }

sections.title: END JSON
sections.content: Dataset: Prompt E.3: Prompt for Differentiative Concept Extraction stage dataset : LIBERTY Role: You are an analyst extracting dataset-level features for medical condition differentiation. Input: You will receive dataset of patient cases. Each item contains: patient_description: free-text description of patients symptoms disease: one of the labels "MIGRAINE", "SINUSITIS", or "INFLUENZA" (these labels were assigned previously) You are also provided with set of existing SYMPTOMS and their descriptions. Task: Symptom Refinement and Optional Augmentation Your primary task is to critically review the provided dataset and determine whether the existing symptoms are sufficiently comprehensive to effectively and completely distinguish between MIGRAINE, SINUSITIS, and INFLUENZA across the entire dataset. If and ONLY IF the existing symptoms are not sufficient, you may augment them by extracting additional symptoms from the dataset. Important: You are NOT required to add new symptoms. You may add FEWER than "B#" symptoms. You may add ZERO new symptoms if the existing set is already sufficient. Adding unnecessary or redundant symptoms is worse than adding none. Only extract additional symptoms if they provide clear and meaningful improvement in distinguishing between the diseases. Definition of Symptom: Symptom is concrete, clinically meaningful manifestation explicitly described in patient text (e.g., headache, fever, nausea, nasal congestion). Symptoms must be grounded in explicit textual evidence and must generalize across multiple patients. Requirements: Differentiation: Each newly added symptom must clearly help distinguish between at least two of the diseases. For every added symptom, it must be true that the symptom is more frequent, more prominent, or more characteristic in one disease than in at least one of the other diseases. Necessity: Only add symptom if its absence would leave meaningful gap in disease differentiation. Do NOT add symptoms that are redundant with existing ones or that add minimal new information. Classification usefulness: All symptoms (existing and newly added) must be usable for classifying new, unseen patient descriptions whose disease label is unknown. Count: You may add up to "B#" additional symptoms. You may add fewer than "B#" symptoms, or none at all. Do NOT force the number of added symptoms to reach "B#". Naming: Give each symptom short, precise, clinically meaningful name. Avoid vague or abstract names. Do not include disease names in symptom names. Avoid trivial or circular symptoms: Do not restate disease labels. Do not add overly specific or one-off symptoms that do not generalize across patients. Output format (strict): Your output must contain two sections, in this exact order. Section 1 Reasoning: Briefly explain (13 sentences per symptom) whether the existing symptoms are sufficient. If new symptoms are added, explain why each added symptom is necessary and what differentiating gap it fills. If no new symptoms are added, explicitly state that the existing symptoms are sufficient. Section 2 Symptoms JSON: Output single valid JSON object suitable for saving as .json file. The JSON must include: All existing symptoms (unchanged) Any newly added symptoms (if applicable) Each key is symptom name. Each value is concise description explaining how the symptom differs across MIGRAINE, SINUSITIS, and/or INFLUENZA. Do not add comments. Do not add extra fields. After the JSON object is closed, print exactly: END JSON Toy Example (for illustration only): Existing Symptoms (provided to the model): Localization: Describes where symptoms are primarily experienced in the body. Toy Input Dataset (maximum number of symptoms to add: 2): Item 1: patient_description: "Throbbing headache on one side with nausea and light sensitivity." disease: MIGRAINE Item 2: patient_description: "Facial pressure, congestion, and pain around the cheeks for several days." disease: SINUSITIS Item 3: patient_description: "Sudden fever, muscle aches, cough, and extreme fatigue." disease: INFLUENZA Explanation of insufficiency (implicit in reasoning): The existing symptom Localization captures where symptoms occur but does not capture differences in whole-body involvement or temporal onset. Toy Output (number of symptoms added: 2): Reasoning: Localization: Symptoms are concentrated in different body regions across diseases, but this alone does not capture differences in systemic involvement. SystemicSymptoms: Some diseases involve widespread bodily effects such as fatigue and aches, which are not captured by localization alone. OnsetSpeed: Descriptions differ in whether symptoms begin suddenly or develop gradually, providing an additional distinguishing signal. { "Localization": "Symptoms differ in where discomfort is concentrated across diseases.", "SystemicSymptoms": "Some diseases involve widespread bodily symptoms, while others are primarily localized.", "OnsetSpeed": "Symptoms differ in whether they appear suddenly or develop over time." } END JSON Dataset: Previous Symptoms: Prompt E.4: Prompt for Concept Annotation stage dataset : LIBERTY Role: You are symptom-level medical classification annotator. Input: You will receive: dataset of patient cases. Each item contains: item_id: an integer representing the items original index patient_description: free-text description of patients symptoms list of SYMPTOMS. Each symptom includes: symptom_name: short, precise name symptom_description: description explaining what the symptom captures as clinical manifestation Task: For each dataset item, assign label to each SYMPTOM based on: Whether the symptom appears in the patient_description at all. If it appears, which disease or diseases the symptom is most consistent with, given the context of the description. Disease set: MIGRAINE SINUSITIS INFLUENZA Label definitions (important read carefully): 0 The symptom does NOT appear in the text at all. There is no explicit mention or clear reference to this symptom in the patient_description. 1 The symptom appears in the text and fits MIGRAINE only. 2 The symptom appears in the text and fits SINUSITIS only. 3 The symptom appears in the text and fits INFLUENZA only. 4 The symptom appears in the text and fits BOTH MIGRAINE and SINUSITIS. 5 The symptom appears in the text and fits BOTH MIGRAINE and INFLUENZA. 6 The symptom appears in the text and fits BOTH SINUSITIS and INFLUENZA. 7 The symptom appears in the text, BUT it does NOT meaningfully differentiate between MIGRAINE, SINUSITIS, and INFLUENZA. Use this label ONLY when the symptom is explicitly mentioned and is equally consistent with all three diseases given the description. Important rules: Assign exactly one label (07) for every symptom in every item. Label 0 must be used ONLY when the symptom is completely absent from the text. If the symptom appears even vaguely, label 0 is NOT allowed. Label 7 must be used ONLY when the symptom appears in the text AND cannot be used to distinguish between the three diseases. Use the symptom_description together with the patient_description to decide whether the symptom is present, and whether it differentiates between diseases or not. symptom may appear regardless of the final diagnosed disease. Do NOT infer the presence of symptom unless it is explicitly supported by the text. Disease compatibility should be judged based on how the symptom is described (e.g., location, quality, duration, context), not on the dataset label. If symptom clearly fits multiple diseases, select the label corresponding to the full set of diseases it fits. If it fits all three equally, select label 7. Output format (strict): Your output must contain two sections, in this exact order. Section 1 Reasoning: For each dataset item, briefly explain the reasoning behind the assigned labels. The reasoning should reference: evidence from the patient_description why each symptom was considered present or absent why the symptom does or does not differentiate between diseases Keep explanations concise (12 sentences per symptom per item). Do NOT include JSON in this section. Section 2 Item Symptom Output (JSON): Output single valid JSON object suitable for saving as .json file. JSON rules: Each key is the item_id converted to string. Each value is an object whose keys are the symptom_name values and whose values are strings in {0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7}. All symptoms must appear for every item. Do not add extra fields or comments. After the JSON object is closed, print exactly: END JSON Dataset: Prompt E.5: Prompt for Preliminary Expansion stage dataset : LAJ Role: You are preference optimization and data alignment expert. Core Task: Your goal is to make the concept: "*Target Concept*" associate with the "*direction*" Response. The description of "*Target Concept*": *Concept description* For EACH item, strictly follow this: Check "*Target Concept*" Current Label. Based ONLY on this label: IF Current Label IS "*wanted label*": DO NOT CHANGE Chosen Response or Rejected Response. ChangeFlag = False. IF Current Label IS NOT "*wanted label*": You MUST MODIFY both Chosen Response and/or Rejected Response. Modification Goal: Make Chosen Response "*directionChosen*" aligned with *Target Concept*, and make Rejected Response "*directionRejected*" aligned with *Target Concept*. Guidelines for Mandatory Modifications: * Plausibility: Responses must remain logical and coherent. * Subtlety (if possible): Aim for minimal effective changes. ChangeFlag = True. Reasoning (MANDATORY if modified): Explain the exact text changes and how they make "*Target Concept*" fit Chosen Response and Rejected Response. Input: Dataset items, each with Original Index, User Query, Chosen Response, Rejected Response, and Concept Labels (telling you the Current Label for *Target Concept*). Output Format: 1. JSON Output: single valid JSON object. Item keys are Item + Original Index (e.g., Item98). JSON Structure: { "Item_Original_Index_Here" : { "User Query": "Original user query", "Original Index": "Original Index from input", "Chosen Response": "Final Chosen Response", "Rejected Response": "Final Rejected Response", "ChangeFlag": boolean } } END JSON 2. Reasoning Output (After JSON, one block per item): Reasoning for Item [Original Index]: 1. Original Index: [Original Index] 2. Target Concept: [*Target Concept* Name] 3. Current Label for Target Concept: [1, 0, 2, or 3] 4. Target Label: "*wanted label*" 5. Critical Check Outcome: ["Labels are identical (Current Label is "*wanted label*")" OR "Labels are NOT identical (Current Label is [actual_label], not "*wanted label*")"] 6. Scenario Applied: ["Scenario applied (because Current Label is "*wanted label*")" OR "Scenario applied (because Current Label is [actual_label], not "*wanted label*")"] 7. Final Action & ChangeFlag Value Details: - If Scenario A: "No modifications made as required by Scenario A. ChangeFlag is False." - If Scenario B: "Modifications were mandatory under Scenario B. [Describe changes to Chosen and Rejected responses.]" ChangeFlag is True. 8. If you were given the reason for current labeling by the LLM, add how you included this reason in the modified responses. EXAMPLE (Keep this output structure and reasoning detail): Lets assume the Target Concept is Conciseness. Input Item 1: Original Index: 98 User Query: What is the capital of France? Chosen Response: Ah, youre asking about the capital of France! Paris Well, let me tell you, the truly magnificent, beautiful, and significantly historic capital city of the glorious French Republic, which is situated in the lovely north-central part of that European country, is none other than Paris, global center for art, fashion, gastronomy and culture. Rejected Response: Paris. Concept Labels: {"Conciseness": 3} Input Item 2: Original Index: 99 User Query: Explain photosynthesis briefly. Chosen Response: Plants make food from sun. Rejected Response: Photosynthesis is the process plants use to convert light energy into chemical energy (food), using water, carbon dioxide, and sunlight, releasing oxygen as byproduct. Its vital for life on Earth. Concept Labels: {"Conciseness": 1} Expected JSON Output: { "Item98": { "User Query": "What is the capital of France?", "Original Index": "98", "Chosen Response": "The beautiful and historic capital city of the French Republic, located in the north-central part of the country, is Paris.", "Rejected Response": "The beautiful and historic Paris.", "ChangeFlag": true }, "Item99": { "User Query": "Explain photosynthesis briefly.", "Original Index": "99", "Chosen Response": "Plants make food from sun.", "Rejected Response": "Photosynthesis is the process...", "ChangeFlag": false } } END JSON Expected Reasoning Output: Reasoning for Item 98: 1. Original Index: 98 2. Target Concept: Conciseness 3. Current Label for Target Concept: 3 4. Target Label: 1 5. Critical Check Outcome: Labels are NOT identical (Current Label is 3, not 1). 6. Scenario Applied: Scenario applied (because Current Label is 3, not 1). 7. Final Action & ChangeFlag Value Details: Modifications were mandatory under Scenario B. The original Rejected Response ("Paris.") was made significantly less concise by adding verbiage. The original Chosen Response (a long sentence) was made more concise. These changes aim to ensure "Conciseness" now better represents Chosen. ChangeFlag is True. Reasoning for Item 99: 1. Original Index: 99 2. Target Concept: Conciseness 3. Current Label for Target Concept: 1 4. Target Label: 1 5. Critical Check Outcome: Labels are identical (Current Label is 1). 6. Scenario Applied: Scenario applied (because Current Label is 1). 7. Final Action & ChangeFlag Value Details: No modifications made as required by Scenario A. ChangeFlag is False. Dataset: Prompt E.6: Prompt for MCMC Inspired Expansion stage dataset : LIBERTY Role: You are medical data augmentation and modification expert. Core Task: Your goal is to modify patient description so that symptom: *SYMPTOM* becomes *DX* aligned with *DISEASE*. Target Symptom description: *SYMPTOM* : *SYMPTOM_DESCRIPTION* Symptom labels and their meanings: 0 The symptom does NOT appear in the text at all. There is no explicit mention or clear reference to this symptom in the patient_description. 1 The symptom appears in the text and fits MIGRAINE only. 2 The symptom appears in the text and fits SINUSITIS only. 3 The symptom appears in the text and fits INFLUENZA only. 4 The symptom appears in the text and fits BOTH MIGRAINE and SINUSITIS. 5 The symptom appears in the text and fits BOTH MIGRAINE and INFLUENZA. 6 The symptom appears in the text and fits BOTH SINUSITIS and INFLUENZA. 7 The symptom appears in the text, BUT it does NOT meaningfully differentiate between MIGRAINE, SINUSITIS, and INFLUENZA. Use this label ONLY when the symptom is explicitly mentioned and is equally consistent with all three diseases. For EACH item, strictly follow this: You MUST MODIFY the patient_description if alignment is requested. Modification Goal: Make the patient_description *DX* aligned with the Target Symptom for the *DISEASE* disease. Critical Rule NO DISEASE MENTION (MANDATORY): You MUST NOT mention, suggest, imply, or hint at the disease name or diagnosis in the patient_description. Do NOT use disease names (e.g., migraine, sinusitis, flu). Do NOT use phrases that directly suggest diagnosis (e.g., consistent with migraine, suggestive of flu). Alignment must be achieved ONLY through symptom characteristics (e.g., location, quality, duration, triggers, severity). Guidelines for Mandatory Modifications: Plausibility: The modified patient_description must remain medically realistic and coherent. Subtlety (if possible): Aim for minimal but effective textual changes. Symptom isolation: Make strong effort NOT to change the presence or interpretation of other symptoms. Do NOT introduce new unrelated symptoms unless absolutely necessary. ChangeFlag must be set to True if any modification is made. Reasoning (MANDATORY if modified): You must explain: What exact textual changes were made How those changes increase or decrease alignment of the Target Symptom with the *DISEASE* disease Why the modification respects the label definitions above Confirm explicitly that no disease name or diagnosis was mentioned in the patient_description Input: Dataset items, each containing: Original Index, patient_description, and the Current label for the target Symptom (07). Output Format: JSON Output: Output single valid JSON object. Item keys are Item + Original Index (e.g., Item42). JSON structure: { "Item_Original_Index_Here": { "Original_index": "Original Index from input", "Chosen Disease": "MIGRAINE SINUSITIS INFLUENZA", "Target Symptom": "Target Symptom name", "Original Patient Description": "Original description", "Modified Patient Description": "Final description", "ChangeFlag": true } } END JSON Reasoning Output (after JSON, one block per item): Reasoning for Item [Original Index]: Original Index: [Original Index] Target Symptom: [Target Symptom] Current Label: [0--7] Chosen Disease: [Disease] Desired Direction: [MORE aligned / LESS aligned] Final Action: Describe the precise textual edits and explain how they change the alignment of the Target Symptom with the chosen disease without mentioning or implying the disease, while keeping other symptoms stable. EXAMPLE (fully worked example): Input values for this example: Target Symptom: Headache Symptom description: Describes the presence and characteristics of head pain. Chosen disease: MIGRAINE Target alignment: MORE aligned Current Label: 0 Original patient_description: Patient reports mild fatigue and nausea over the past day, but no other significant complaints. Explanation of the setup: The current label is 0, meaning headache does not appear at all in the original text. Target alignment = MORE aligned and DISEASE = MIGRAINE. The description must be modified to introduce headache pattern typical of migraine without mentioning or suggesting migraine. Other symptoms (fatigue, nausea) must remain unchanged. Expected JSON Output: { "42": { "Original_index": "42", "Chosen Disease": "MIGRAINE", "Target Symptom": "Headache", "Original Patient Description": "Patient reports mild fatigue and nausea over the past day, but no other significant complaints.", "Modified Patient Description": "Patient reports mild fatigue and nausea over the past day, along with throbbing headache on one side of the head that worsens with light exposure.", "ChangeFlag": true } } END JSON Expected Reasoning Output: Reasoning for Item 42: Original Index: 42 Target Symptom: Headache Current Label: 0 Chosen Disease: MIGRAINE Desired Direction: MORE aligned Final Action: unilateral, throbbing headache with light sensitivity was added to the patient_description, which increases alignment of the headache symptom with migraine-related patterns. No disease names or diagnostic language were introduced, and existing symptoms were preserved unchanged, fully respecting the label definitions. Dataset:

sections.title: Compute Cost
sections.content: Hardware Infrastructure Experiments evaluating the open-weight models (Qwen3-14B and gpt-OSS-20b) were conducted on an institutional remote compute cluster equipped with NVIDIA H200 and RTX6K GPUs. To optimize inference throughput during the intensive data expansion phase, these models were served utilizing the vLLM (Kwon et al., 2023) framework. Gemini-2Flash was accessed externally via its official API. Cost Estimation The primary computational bottleneck in our proposed framework is the MCMCinspired counterfactual data expansion, which necessitates repeated, iterative LLM sampling. API Costs (Gemini): Executing the complete concept extraction and MCMC expansion pipeline via the Gemini API incurred total cost of approximately $540 USD. Local GPU Compute (Qwen3 & gpt-OSS): Generating the counterfactuals for the openweight models on our institutional cluster incurred negligible marginal compute costs (under $5 USD in equivalent compute time). Importantly, once the counterfactual datasets are generated, the downstream causal discovery phase training the multinomial logistic regression models to extract the graph topologies is computationally lightweight and can be executed on standard CPU infrastructure within minutes.

sections.title: H AI Assistance Statement
sections.content: In accordance with the ACL guidelines regarding the use of AI, we acknowledge the use of AI assistants during the preparation of this work. Specifically, GitHub Copilot was utilized to assist in writing data visualization scripts and refactoring portions of the codebase for asynchronous execution. Additionally, AI language models (Gemini, ChatGPT, and Claude) were used as writing assistants to refine phrasing and improve readability. The authors take full responsibility for all content, ideas, and code presented in this paper. In compliance with reproducibility and ethical guidelines regarding artifact licenses, we detail the licensing, terms of use, and intended usage of the models and datasets utilized in this study. All computational artifacts were employed strictly for academic research purposes, consistent with their respective licenses. Models Gemini 2 Flash (Team et al., 2025): Accessed via the official API. Usage complies with Googles Terms of Service and generative AI usage guidelines for research and evaluation purposes. Qwen3-14B (Yang et al., 2025): The model weights and inference code are utilized under the Apache 2.0 License, which explicitly permits academic research applications. gpt-OSS-20b (OpenAI, 2025): Utilized under its respective open-source license Apache 2.0, adhering to all research usage constraints.

sections.title: Datasets
sections.content: LIBERTY (Disease Diagnosis) (Toker et al., 2026): synthetic medical corpus. The dataset is publicly released by the authors for research purposes; while no explicit license is designated in the original publication, it is utilized here strictly for non-commercial academic research. IMDB (Sentiment Analysis) (Maas et al., 2011): The dataset is publicly available for research purposes and is utilized in accordance with its standard academic distribution terms. LAJ (LLM-as-a-Judge) (Calderon et al., 2025): Derived from Reddit data, the dataset is publicly released by the authors for research purposes. While no explicit open-source license is provided in the original publication, our usage strictly complies with the Reddit API terms of service and is restricted entirely to non-commercial academic research. Code and Framework The code for our concept extraction, MCMC-based counterfactual generation, and causal graph construction is open-sourced and released under the MIT License to facilitate unrestricted use and reproducibility by the research community.
